worth_it_explanation: Unified ID 2.0 (UID2) and European Unified ID (EUID) tokens let a publisher's authenticated audience stay addressable in the bid stream as third-party cookies fall away, so the tokens carry real revenue weight on logged-in inventory. The same tokens are built from a person's email or phone, which means generating one without a legal basis turns an addressability gain into a privacy exposure. This check is a review of how the OpenX integration is wired and gated, so it costs reading time rather than rebuild time, and the finding points to a concrete setting the publisher can correct.
intro: UID2 and its European counterpart EUID are identity tokens built from a logged-in user's email or phone, passed in the bid stream so buyers can address an audience without a third-party cookie. On OpenX, the integration is often orchestrated through OpenAudience, OpenX's identity and audience graph, alongside UID2, EUID, and other identifiers. This subcategory covers whether those tokens are generated and passed where a legal basis exists, and whether the generation is gated on consent so a token is never minted without one. The two questions sit together because addressability and lawful basis are the two halves of the same decision.

audit_checks.title: UID2 or EUID tokens are not generated and passed where a legal basis exists
audit_checks.impact_rationale: On logged-in inventory where a lawful basis exists, a missing UID2 or EUID token leaves the impression addressable only by cookie, which buyers increasingly cannot use, so the impression competes for less demand than it could. The gap suppresses bid density and clearing price on exactly the authenticated audience the publisher worked to build, though it is a revenue trade rather than a breach.

educate.base:
Unified ID 2.0 (UID2) is an identity framework that turns a logged-in user's email or phone number into a token a buyer can recognize in the bid stream, so an authenticated audience stays addressable as third-party cookies disappear. European Unified ID (EUID) is the European counterpart, built on the same design but governed for the stricter data-protection rules of the European Union and the United Kingdom. The raw identifier is normalized and hashed before a token is produced, so the original email or phone is never exposed to the buyer who reads the token.

On OpenX, the publisher's side of this is the generator role. The publisher, or a service acting for the publisher, calls a UID2 or EUID operator to mint the token, then carries it into the bid stream. Because OpenX runs a Prebid-compatible header bidding wrapper, the common path is the Prebid user-ID module for UID2, which places the token where bidders can read it. OpenX also offers OpenAudience, its own identity and audience graph, which can orchestrate UID2, EUID, and other identifiers together rather than wiring each one separately.

When the token is absent on inventory where it could lawfully be present, the impression falls back to whatever addressability remains, which for many buyers is now thin. The authenticated audience the publisher built through logins, registrations, or a newsletter then competes in the auction as if it were anonymous traffic, and the bid density and clearing price reflect that. The addressability is available and lawful, but it is not reaching the buyers who would pay for it.

A few signs point to tokens not being generated and passed where they could be:
  • Logged-in or registered users exist, but no UID2 or EUID module is configured in the OpenX or Prebid setup
  • Reporting shows little or no bid activity tied to the UID2 or EUID token on authenticated inventory
  • The publisher has a consented, logged-in audience but the token never appears in the bid request
You may want to confirm that UID2 or EUID tokens are generated and passed on the inventory where an authenticated, consented audience exists. One approach is to trace a logged-in session through the bid request and check whether the token is present, then read whether buyers are bidding on it.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site, the authenticated audience usually comes from registrations, subscriptions, or a newsletter login, which is exactly the inventory where a UID2 or EUID token carries the most weight. You may want to confirm the token is generated on the logged-in sections rather than only on the open homepage, so the subscriber audience the publisher monetizes stays addressable to buyers.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content site, logged-in shoppers and account holders are the high-intent audience buyers most want to reach, so a missing token on account or checkout-adjacent pages is the costliest gap. You may want to confirm UID2 or EUID generation covers the authenticated shopping sessions, where the audience signal and the clearing price are both highest.

educate.learn_more_note: OpenX's detailed configuration documentation for OpenAudience and its UID2 or EUID integration is in the partner portal (login required).

investigate.intro: Review whether a UID2 or EUID token is generated on authenticated inventory and reaches the bid stream, and whether buyers are bidding on it.

browser_devtools.label: Browser DevTools
steps: Open the site as a logged-in user, open DevTools, and watch the 'Network' tab for the header bidding requests.
Inspect a bid request and look for the UID2 or EUID token in the user-ID section of the payload, confirming whether it is present on the authenticated page.
Repeat on a page where the user is not logged in, and confirm the token is absent there, which separates the authenticated path from the open path.
Record which sections of the site carry the token in the bid request and which do not.

openx_reporting.label: OpenX reporting
steps: Ask the publisher to break out bid activity by whether a UID2 or EUID token was present on the request.
Compare bid density and clearing price on token-present impressions against token-absent impressions for the same authenticated inventory.
Note whether the authenticated inventory carries the token at all, and whether buyers are bidding on it when it is present.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per section of authenticated inventory, whether a UID2 or EUID token is generated and present in the bid request, and whether buyers are bidding on it. Inventory where an authenticated, consented audience exists and the token is generated and passing buyers passes. Inventory where a logged-in, consented audience exists but no token reaches the bid stream is a fail recorded with the sections affected.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record whether a UID2 or EUID token is present in the bid request on each authenticated section above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} has an authenticated, consented audience but is not generating or passing Unified ID 2.0 (UID2) or European Unified ID (EUID) tokens on that inventory, so the logged-in audience competes in the auction as if it were anonymous. With third-party cookies fading, a missing token leaves the impression addressable only by signals many buyers can no longer use, which suppresses bid density and clearing price on exactly the audience {{site_name}} built. {{voice_recommend}} configuring UID2 and EUID token generation on the authenticated inventory, through the OpenX OpenAudience orchestration or the Prebid user-ID module, so the token reaches the bid stream where a legal basis exists. After the change, confirm the token appears in the bid request and that buyers are bidding on it.

audit_checks.title: Token generation is not gated on a valid legal basis
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A UID2 or EUID token is derived from a person's email or phone, so generating one without a lawful basis processes personal data without permission, which is a privacy and legal exposure under the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) and similar laws. The breach scales with every logged-in session, and the European Unified ID design exists specifically because the European market requires the consent gate, so a missing gate puts the publisher on the wrong side of the rule it was built to satisfy.

educate.base:
A Unified ID 2.0 (UID2) or European Unified ID (EUID) token is not anonymous data. It is derived from a person's email address or phone number, normalized and hashed into a token that buyers can match against their own records. Because the input is directly identifying, the act of generating the token is the processing of personal data, and that processing needs a lawful basis. In the European Union and the United Kingdom, that basis runs through the GDPR and the consent signals the Transparency and Consent Framework (TCF) carries. EUID exists as a separate framework from UID2 precisely because the European rules are stricter, and its design assumes the consent gate is in place.

The gate works by reading the user's consent state before any token is minted. A consent management platform (CMP) collects the user's choices and exposes them, and the integration checks that signal first. Where consent for the relevant purposes is present, the token is generated and passed. Where it is absent or refused, no token is generated, and the impression proceeds without one. The decision happens before generation rather than after. A token that should never have existed cannot be cleanly recalled once it is in the bid stream.

A gate that is missing, miswired, or applied only after the token is already minted defeats its purpose. If the integration generates the token for every logged-in session and then relies on downstream filtering, personal data has already been processed for the users who refused, and the exposure is real regardless of what happens next. The audit question is whether generation is conditioned on a valid consent signal at the point of generation, for the markets where consent is the required basis.

A few signs point to generation that is not gated correctly:
  • No CMP consent check sits in front of UID2 or EUID token generation, so tokens are minted for every logged-in user
  • The consent signal is read after the token is generated rather than before, so refusal does not prevent generation
  • European traffic is handled by the UID2 path rather than the EUID path that the European rules require
You may want to confirm that token generation reads a valid consent signal before it mints anything, and that European traffic runs on the EUID framework. One approach is to set a refused consent state in the CMP, then trace a logged-in session and confirm no token is generated.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site, registration and newsletter flows collect the email that feeds the token, so the consent captured at sign-up and the CMP signal both bear on whether generation is lawful. You may want to confirm the consent state from the CMP gates generation at request time, not only the broad terms a reader accepted at registration. The two are separate signals.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content site, account creation and checkout collect contact details for order fulfillment, which is a different purpose from advertising identity. You may want to confirm that an email collected to complete a purchase is not fed into UID2 or EUID generation without a separate advertising consent that the CMP signal reflects.

educate.learn_more_note: OpenX's detailed configuration documentation for wiring the CMP consent signal into OpenAudience token generation is in the partner portal (login required).

investigate.intro: Review whether a valid consent signal is checked before a UID2 or EUID token is generated, and whether European traffic runs on the EUID framework.

browser_devtools.label: Browser DevTools
steps: Open the CMP and set a refused or no-consent state for the advertising purposes, then load the site as a logged-in user.
In the DevTools 'Network' tab, inspect the bid request and confirm no UID2 or EUID token is present, which shows generation respected the refusal.
Set a consented state, reload, and confirm the token now appears, which shows generation is conditioned on the signal rather than always on or always off.
For European traffic, confirm the EUID path is used rather than the UID2 path, and record which framework handles which market.

cmp_review.label: Consent signal review
steps: Ask the publisher how the CMP consent signal is passed into the UID2 or EUID integration, and whether the check sits before or after token generation.
Confirm the consent purposes that gate generation match the basis the integration relies on for the relevant markets.
Record any case where token generation runs ahead of the consent check, or where European traffic is not routed through EUID.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records whether a valid consent signal is read before token generation, confirmed by a refused-consent test that produces no token, and whether European traffic runs on the EUID framework. A setup where generation is conditioned on consent at the point of generation, and European traffic uses EUID, passes. A setup where tokens are generated regardless of consent state, or where the consent check sits after generation, or where European traffic runs on UID2 instead of EUID, is a fail recorded with the gap that breaks the gate.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record whether a refused consent state prevents token generation, and whether European traffic uses EUID, above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} generates Unified ID 2.0 (UID2) or European Unified ID (EUID) tokens without gating that generation on a valid consent signal. A token is derived from a person's email or phone, so minting one without a lawful basis processes personal data without permission, which is an exposure under the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) and the consent rules the European Unified ID framework was built to satisfy. {{voice_recommend}} placing the consent management platform (CMP) check ahead of token generation so no token is minted for a user who has refused, and routing European traffic through the EUID path rather than UID2. After the change, set a refused consent state and confirm no token reaches the bid stream, which proves the gate holds.
